extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A model‑driven Digital Twin (DT) for building infrastructure in the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, built upon an enriched Building Information Modelling (BIM) asset. The system links a static 3D BIM representation to real‑time data streams from sensors, actuators, and controllers deployed in the physical building. Key components include the BIM data repository, device‑modeling extensions that embed sensor/actuator definitions, a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) pipeline that translates the enriched BIM into deployable run‑time interfaces, runtime connectivity via Eclipse Hono/Ditto, and an interface for monitoring, control, and visualization. The DT is designed for scalable, repeatable deployment and supports the building’s full life cycle from design to operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin delivers a suite of services centered on real‑time monitoring, simulation, and optimization of building performance, coupled with control capabilities and immersive visualization. Target users include Building Control Engineers—who configure and analyze device behavior; Facility Managers—who oversee operational states and interact with the twin for day‑to‑day control; and DT Engineers—who extend or customize the twin. Key service capabilities are: (1) live status tracking of assets; (2) model‑based simulation and analysis integrated with sensor data; (3) lifecycle‑wide optimization suggestions; (4) direct actuator control through the twin; and (5) interactive, immersive visualization for monitoring and scenario exploration.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline that processes BIM models enriched with sensor, actuator, and controller metadata to generate deployable runtime artifacts. Real‑time data exchange is achieved through the Eclipse Hono server, which ingests events from physical devices, and the Eclipse Ditto service, which manages real‑time asset representations and state synchronization. The overall architecture is modular, exposing standardized extension points that enable DT Engineers to plug in custom services or analytics without altering core components. The framework is built atop open‑source components (e.g., Eclipse Hono, Ditto) and conforms to industry standards for data modeling and interoperability in the built‑environment sector.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The digital twin is coupled to the physical building through a real‑time integration channel implemented with Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto. State changes in the physical assets are communicated continuously to the twin and control commands from the twin are sent back to the physical system, ensuring that the twin’s state remains tightly synchronized. The architecture is designed to handle both high‑frequency monitoring and event‑driven control loops, with internal heartbeat exchanges that keep the twin responsive while maintaining latencies well below the sub‑second threshold required for real‑time building operations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline where enriched BIM models are parsed and transformed into runtime artifacts.  A central “DT Framework” hub orchestrates the twin lifecycle, providing standardized extension points for custom services, integration of real‑time data streams, and runtime configuration management.  Key actors—Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, and DT Engineer—interact with this core hub to deploy, monitor, and extend the twins, while modular components (Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and custom plugins) are instantiated and managed by the framework.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The twin integrates with external systems through real‑time data pipelines established by Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, which provide a standardized MQTT‑based messaging abstraction for bidirectional communication. External infrastructure or enterprise applications can ingest or publish twin data via the Ditto Thing Registry, supporting interoperability with legacy building management systems and other DTs. The integration layer exposes RESTful endpoints and WebSocket streams for visualization tools, and it allows plug‑in connectors to be added by DT Engineers to link the twin with additional enterprise services such as asset management platforms or maintenance workflow systems. No specific proprietary protocols are mandated; the framework relies on open, interoperable standards to accommodate heterogeneous devices and data sources.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models comprise a hierarchical stack of BIM‑derived geometric models, semantically enriched domain models, and a runtime twin representation. During the one‑click deployment, an IFC BIM enriched with custom device families and property sets is uploaded to the platform. An IFC‑specific model checker validates mandatory attributes such as an Instance General UUID and IFC type (e.g., IFCLIGHTFIXTURETYPE). The verified model is fed into a Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation pipeline that extracts a domain‑specific structured representation, stored in Eclipse Ditto as twin entities. Real‑time data exchange occurs through Eclipse Hono, which forwards timestamps and sensor readings to Ditto, enabling bidirectional synchronization. Interactive visualisation is realised by streaming the twin state to Unreal Engine 5 via a TwinComponent bridge that renders the geometric model and updates visual attributes according to live telemetry.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators in the twin are represented by TwinComponents (e.g., LightTwinComponent) that expose device commands through the UnrealTwinController. Each actuator is addressed by its DittoDevice identifier derived from the device’s IFC Instance General UUID. The twin supports control capabilities such as on/off, brightness percentage (0 %–100 %), and colour‑temperature in Kelvin for luminaires. Remote control is implemented via the Ditto message broker, where the DittoController forwards command payloads to the physical device. Safety is enforced through Eclipse Ditto’s policy‑based validation that checks value ranges and units for actuator commands and performs pre‑execution conflict checks. Fault tolerance is handled by Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once delivery semantics and Ditto’s persistent event storage, ensuring that command updates survive transient network outages.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure is modelled inline with devices in the BIM environment by custom property sets, then exported in IFC. Once the IFC is uploaded, the model checker verifies required sensor metadata (e.g., Instance General UUID, IFC type). The internal twin stores each sensor as a DittoDevice, which publishes measurement values to Hono. Sensors feed data at real‑time rates, backed by a 1‑second heartbeat mechanism that triggers periodic state updates. The heartbeat synchronises sensor reads and ensures low‑latency telemetry. In the twin, sensor values are validated against policy‑enforced ranges, units, and precision before propagation to the visualisation engine. Spatial distribution follows the BIM topology; each sensor inherits its location from the geometry in the IFC model.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: System fidelity is quantified through the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric, comprising timeliness (0.997), reliability (1.0), and availability (1.0). The ODTE value of 0.997 indicates near‑real‑time coupling. Validation and verification occur in the model‑checking phase, where the IFC rule engine enforces the presence of a mandatory Instance General UUID and IFC type. Runtime validation is performed by Eclipse Ditto’s policy engine, which checks data ranges, units, and conflict avoidance. Fault tolerance is achieved via Eclipse Hono’s at‑least‑once message delivery and Ditto’s event persistence, guaranteeing no loss of critical state changes. The twin’s state continuity is guaranteed by the periodic 1‑second heartbeat that triggers updates, while the model‑to‑runtime mapping preserves congruence between static BIM attributes and dynamic sensor data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Telemetry from physical building sensors and actuators is transmitted to the Eclipse Hono gateway (configured via the Hono/Ditto Configurator). Hono forwards the data, typically encoded as JSON payloads, to the Eclipse Ditto twin store. Ditto then makes the updated twin state available to client applications. The Unreal Engine 5 (UE5) runtime receives an initial batch of device values and parameters through an HTTP POST request to a Ditto HTTP endpoint. After initialisation, the UE5 client establishes a WebSocket connection to Ditto to receive continuous telemetry updates in real time. The data flow is continuous, triggered by sensor value changes, relying on Ditto’s subscription mechanism. No additional preprocessing steps are described beyond JSON payload handling by Hono and Ditto.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Commands generated by Facility Managers through the UE5 interface are sent by UE5 as WebSocket messages to Eclipse Ditto, which forwards them to the Hono gateway. Hono then routes the command to the target device using the device’s endpoint address encoded in its metadata (tenant, device ID). The command payload is a JSON object containing the desired state or action (e.g., turn on a light, adjust temperature). Before committing to a new twin state, the system waits for a confirmation acknowledgement from the device, ensuring that the physical device successfully executed the command. The device responds back through Hono/Ditto, and the UE5 client updates the twin display after receipt of confirmation. Conflict resolution is implicit in that the twin state is only updated after successful execution acknowledgement.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical connection between the physical building, the twin infrastructure, and the UE5 client is built upon the Eclipse Hono connectivity framework and Eclipse Ditto twin management service. Hono serves as the messaging broker, exposing MQTT, AMQP, and HTTP APIs to onboard device telemetry and commands. Ditto stores the twin representation and offers a WebSocket endpoint for subscriptions and command delivery. UE5 communicates with Ditto via a secure WebSocket (TLS) channel for low‑latency data exchange, and uses HTTP POST for bootstrapping initial device values. The overall topology is a client–server model where UE5 is the client, and Ditto and Hono form the server stack. While explicit bandwidth, latency or redundancy figures are not provided, the system is designed for real‑time interaction, implying sub‑second round‑trip times and relying on Hono’s gateway clustering and Ditto’s persistent storage for reliability.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The twin lifecycle follows a sequence of enrichment, semantic extraction, automated deployment, runtime operation, and dynamic configuration. Initial BIM models are augmented with device‑level metadata, then semantically transformed into structured runtime artifacts. An automated one‑click deployment engine generates the necessary DT artefacts, establishes connectivity with physical devices via Eclipse Hono/Ditto, and launches the twin. During operation the twin exchanges real‑time, bidirectional telemetry and offers interactive 3D visualisation. Runtime configuration resources enable parameter tuning and interface updates without full redeployment, closing the loop back to the BIM model for future iterations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Twin construction is guided by a model‑driven engineering (MDE) pipeline. Device definitions are encoded into BIM with semantic annotations, then extracted into domain‑specific models. Code generators convert these models into the required runtime artefacts (e.g., Ditto entity definitions and Hono data streams). Automated deployment scripts provision the DT and hook up the physical devices. The system leverages a lightweight state machine for configuration management, allowing adaptations to changing requirements. Validation is achieved through model‑checking of the enriched BIM, at‑least‑once delivery guarantees of Eclipse Hono, and integration tests that confirm bidirectional data flow and visualisation fidelity.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The platform delivers real‑time monitoring and control. Data harvested through Eclipse Hono/Ditto is streamed to a live 3D visualisation rendered with UE5, enabling facility managers to observe current building states and issue control commands. Decision support is limited to these monitoring and control interactions; the system does not perform advanced analytics or confidence scoring within the current implementation.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Interoperability is founded on the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) open BIM format for modelling, with semantic extraction yielding structured runtime models. Connectivity is achieved through Eclipse Hono, an MQTT‑based broker that guarantees at‑least‑once delivery, and Eclipse Ditto, an open‑source digital‑twin engine. The framework implements the NF1 interoperability and NF2 scalability non‑functional requirements, ensuring compatibility across diverse BIM authoring tools, IoT protocols and visualization platforms. No specific certifications or formal compliance frameworks are cited.

models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)

operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
